past_medical_history: Chronic low back pain with two prior lumbar MRIs showing mild degenerative changes
Irritable bowel syndrome diagnosed at age 28
Major depressive disorder diagnosed at age 25, in partial remission
History of childhood physical abuse disclosed during psychiatric evaluation at age 26
Vitamin D deficiency treated with supplementation
Cesarean section at age 34 for breech presentation

medications.frequency: As needed up to three times daily

family_history: Father with type 2 diabetes and hypertension
Maternal aunt with multiple sclerosis diagnosed at age 38
One sibling (sister, age 37) with chronic fatigue syndrome
No known family history of epilepsy, stroke, or neurodegenerative disease

social_history.occupation: Former administrative assistant, on disability leave for 4 months due to progressive weakness
social_history.alcohol: Occasional glass of wine on weekends, approximately 2-3 per month
social_history.tobacco: Quit smoking 6 years ago, previously smoked half a pack per day for 8 years
social_history.recreational_drugs: Denies current use; remote history of marijuana use in her twenties
social_history.living_situation: Lives with her 6-year-old son and her mother who moved in to help with childcare after the patient became wheelchair-bound
social_history.exercise: Previously walked 2-3 miles daily; now wheelchair-dependent for 6 weeks
social_history.recent_stressors: Contentious custody dispute with ex-husband over past 8 months; lost her job 5 months ago

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Affect mildly flat, appears fatigued. Speech fluent with normal rate, rhythm, and prosody. No paraphasic errors. Naming, repetition, and comprehension intact. Attention intact on digit span (6 forward, 4 backward). Memory 3/3 registration and 2/3 at 5-minute recall (recalled third item with category cue). Clock drawing normal. No evidence of thought disorder or psychotic features.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, reactive to light 3 mm to 2 mm bilaterally. Visual fields full to confrontation. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. Facial sensation intact bilaterally. Face symmetric at rest and with activation. Hearing intact bilaterally to finger rub. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline without atrophy or fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Upper extremities: Normal bulk and tone. Strength 5/5 throughout in deltoids, biceps, triceps, wrist extensors and flexors, hand intrinsics bilaterally. No pronator drift. Lower extremities: Normal bulk. Tone appears normal passively but the patient reports inability to move either leg voluntarily. On direct manual muscle testing, she exhibits give-way weakness bilaterally — initial strong contraction followed by sudden collapse against resistance in hip flexors, knee extensors, knee flexors, and ankle dorsiflexors bilaterally. When distracted during examination (e.g., testing upper extremity strength while asking her to push down with her feet against the bed), spontaneous hip flexion and knee extension noted bilaterally with near-normal force. No fasciculations, atrophy, or asymmetry observed.
neurological_exam.sensory: Reports complete loss of sensation to light touch, pinprick, temperature, vibration, and proprioception in both legs from the inguinal crease distally, with a sharp non-dermatomal demarcation at the waistline. Notably, the boundary does not follow any peripheral nerve or spinal cord level. Upper extremity sensation fully intact. No extinction on double simultaneous stimulation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric at biceps, triceps, brachioradialis bilaterally. Patellar reflexes 2+ bilaterally. Achilles reflexes 2+ bilaterally. Plantar responses are flexor (downgoing) bilaterally. No clonus.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose without dysmetria bilaterally. Heel-to-shin testing not possible per patient report due to leg weakness, though involuntary leg movements observed during position changes.
neurological_exam.gait: Patient is wheelchair-bound and states she cannot stand. When assisted to standing with two-person support, she demonstrates dramatic buckling of both knees without falling. When she believes she is not being observed (caught on video during nursing assessment), she was noted to shift her legs and reposition herself in the wheelchair using both legs with apparent normal strength.
neurological_exam.additional: Hoover sign: On formal testing, when the patient was asked to flex the right hip against resistance, there was no downward pressure from the contralateral (left) leg. However, when asked to push down with the left leg (ostensibly to test left hip extension), involuntary strong hip flexion of the right leg was clearly felt by the examiner. This was reproducible bilaterally. This constitutes a positive Hoover sign, consistent with functional weakness. Notably, no clear functional signs were detected on initial bedside exam by the admitting team — the Hoover sign was only elicited when specifically sought by the consulting neurologist.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive bilateral leg weakness over the past 8 weeks, now wheelchair-bound, with numbness in both legs from the waist down
patient.history_present_illness:
Ms. Kowalski is a 40-year-old right-handed woman with a history of fibromyalgia, chronic pain, and depression who is admitted for evaluation of progressive bilateral lower extremity weakness that has rendered her wheelchair-dependent over the past six weeks. The symptoms began approximately eight weeks ago with a sense of heaviness in both legs while walking. She initially attributed this to a fibromyalgia flare, as she had been under significant psychosocial stress related to an ongoing custody dispute and recent job loss. Over the following two weeks, the heaviness progressed to frank weakness, and she began stumbling and requiring a cane. By four weeks into the illness, she required a walker, and by six weeks she states she could no longer stand unassisted and transitioned to a wheelchair.

She also reports complete numbness in both legs from the waist down that developed gradually over the same time course. She describes it as though her legs belong to someone else and she cannot feel anything touching them. She denies any bowel or bladder incontinence, though she reports urinary urgency that she attributes to stress. There is no associated back pain radiating into the legs, no history of trauma, and no preceding febrile illness or vaccination. She denies visual changes, diplopia, dysphagia, upper extremity weakness, or facial weakness.

She was initially evaluated by her primary care physician who obtained a lumbar MRI showing only mild degenerative disc disease at L4-L5, unchanged from prior imaging two years ago. Basic blood work including CBC, metabolic panel, and inflammatory markers were within normal limits. She was referred to a community neurologist who performed a brief examination, noted bilateral leg weakness, and was concerned for possible myelopathy or Guillain-Barré syndrome variant. She was urgently referred to the academic medical center for further workup.

Her mother, who has been her primary caregiver, reports that the patient has been emotionally distraught about the custody situation and that the weakness seemed to accelerate after a particularly stressful court hearing five weeks ago. The mother also notes that she has occasionally observed the patient moving her legs while asleep and while repositioning in bed, which she finds inconsistent with the patient's reported inability to move them at all. The patient becomes visibly distressed when these observations are mentioned, stating that her mother must be mistaken.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9.5 Hz well-organized alpha rhythm, symmetric, reactive to eye opening
background.sleep_features: Stage II sleep achieved with vertex waves, sleep spindles, and K-complexes

artifacts.impact: Minimal, intermittent, does not significantly obscure recording

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Three minutes performed. Mild symmetric background slowing, returned to baseline within 90 seconds. No epileptiform discharges provoked.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Normal photic driving response at mid-frequencies. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Normal awake and asleep EEG. No epileptiform discharges. No electrographic seizures captured during this 40-minute routine recording. Normal background activity and sleep architecture.
eeg.limitations: Routine 40-minute recording. No clinical events occurred during the recording to assess for EEG correlate.
recommended_actions: If clinical events (seizure-like episodes) are reported, prolonged video-EEG monitoring recommended to capture events and assess for EEG correlate
Correlate with neuroimaging and clinical examination findings

additional_observations: No mass lesion or pathological enhancement
Unremarkable intracranial vasculature on MRA sequences
mri.impression: Normal brain MRI and MRA. No structural lesion, demyelination, infarction, or mass identified to explain the patient's bilateral lower extremity weakness or sensory loss.
recommended_actions: If myelopathy suspected, MRI of cervical and thoracic spine recommended
Correlate with clinical examination and electrophysiological studies

Autoimmune_Screen.clinical_significance: Rules out neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder

labs.interpretation: All laboratory values within normal limits. CBC, BMP, thyroid function, inflammatory markers, vitamin levels, autoimmune screening, and CK are unremarkable. No laboratory evidence of inflammatory, metabolic, or autoimmune etiology for bilateral lower extremity weakness.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9.5 Hz alpha rhythm, well-organized, symmetric, reactive
background.sleep_features: Normal sleep architecture with all expected sleep elements including vertex waves, K-complexes, sleep spindles, and REM sleep

findings.type: Clinical event captured - bilateral leg shaking episode
findings.frequency: One episode captured at hour 38 of monitoring
findings.morphology: During the event (lasting approximately 3 minutes), the patient exhibited bilateral irregular leg shaking, arching of the back, and eyes tightly shut. EEG remained normal throughout with preserved alpha rhythm and no epileptiform activity. Event was preceded by a phone call from patient's lawyer and visible emotional distress on video.
findings.clinical_correlation: Clinical event without EEG correlate is consistent with a psychogenic non-epileptic seizure (PNES). The irregular, waxing-and-waning character, preserved EEG background, eye closure, and identifiable emotional precipitant strongly support this interpretation.

findings.morphology: Patient observed to reposition legs, pull blankets with feet, and shift lower body during sleep stages verified by EEG. These movements demonstrate preserved motor pathways to bilateral lower extremities.
findings.clinical_correlation: Preservation of voluntary-appearing leg movements during sleep is inconsistent with structural or peripheral neurological weakness and supports a functional etiology.

output.impression: Prolonged 72-hour video-EEG monitoring: Normal EEG throughout. One clinical event captured — bilateral leg shaking episode with back arching, eyes closed, and no EEG correlate — consistent with a psychogenic non-epileptic event (PNES). Patient also demonstrated preserved leg movements during verified sleep, inconsistent with reported complete paralysis. No epileptiform discharges identified throughout the recording.
output.limitations: Only one clinical event captured during the monitoring period.
recommended_actions: Findings are consistent with functional neurological disorder
Multidisciplinary approach including neurology, psychiatry, and physical therapy recommended
Discuss video findings with patient in a supportive, non-confrontational manner

EMG_Needle_Examination.value: Reduced volitional recruitment but MUAP morphology normal
EMG_Needle_Examination.clinical_significance: Reduced recruitment without abnormal MUAP morphology is consistent with submaximal volitional effort rather than neurogenic or myopathic process

EMG_Needle_Examination.value: Reduced volitional recruitment but MUAP morphology normal
EMG_Needle_Examination.clinical_significance: Same pattern as right — submaximal effort without neurogenic or myopathic abnormalities

output.interpretation: Normal nerve conduction studies in bilateral lower extremities. EMG needle examination shows no evidence of denervation, neuropathy, myopathy, or radiculopathy. Reduced volitional recruitment in bilateral lower extremity muscles with normal MUAP morphology is consistent with submaximal effort rather than a neurogenic or myopathic process. These electrodiagnostic findings do not support a structural neuromuscular etiology for the reported bilateral leg weakness.

signal_characteristics.post_gadolinium: No enhancement of the spinal cord or meninges

findings.mass_effect: No significant neural foraminal or spinal canal stenosis

additional_observations: Normal conus medullaris terminating at L1 level
No evidence of myelopathy, intramedullary lesion, or cord compression
No evidence of transverse myelitis or demyelination
Cauda equina nerve roots appear normal
The L4-L5 disc bulge is unchanged from prior imaging and not clinically significant
output.impression: Normal cervical and thoracic spinal cord MRI with contrast. No myelopathy, demyelination, cord compression, or intramedullary lesion. Mild degenerative changes at L4-L5 unchanged from prior, without significant stenosis. No structural spine or spinal cord pathology to explain the patient's bilateral lower extremity weakness or sensory complaints.
recommended_actions: Correlate with clinical examination and electrophysiological findings
Normal spinal cord imaging effectively excludes myelopathy as etiology of bilateral leg weakness

Effort_and_Validity.clinical_significance: Valid effort; above threshold for malingering

Psychological_Scales.reference_range: 0-4 minimal, 5-9 mild, 10-14 moderate, 15-19 moderately severe, 20-27 severe

Psychological_Scales.clinical_significance: Elevated score suggestive of probable PTSD, consistent with reported history of childhood trauma

output.interpretation: Neuropsychological testing reveals average cognitive functioning across all domains with valid effort on performance validity testing. No evidence of cognitive impairment or malingering. However, psychological screening reveals moderately severe depression (PHQ-9 = 18), moderate anxiety (GAD-7 = 14), and elevated PTSD symptoms (PCL-5 = 42) exceeding clinical threshold. These findings are consistent with significant psychiatric comorbidity and support the diagnosis of functional neurological disorder in the context of psychological distress, childhood trauma history, and current psychosocial stressors.

Psychiatric_Assessment.value: Functional Neurological Symptom Disorder (Conversion Disorder) with weakness (F44.4)
Psychiatric_Assessment.clinical_significance: Meets DSM-5 criteria: neurological symptoms incompatible with recognized neurological conditions, with positive clinical signs (Hoover sign, inconsistency between volitional and automatic movements)
Current episode with depressed mood, anhedonia, sleep disturbance, fatigue, and feelings of worthlessness
Related to childhood physical abuse; experiencing hypervigilance, avoidance, intrusive memories, and emotional numbing

Psychiatric_Assessment.value: Low - passive ideation without plan or intent
Psychiatric_Assessment.clinical_significance: Safety plan established; no indication for acute psychiatric admission

output.interpretation: Comprehensive psychiatric evaluation reveals functional neurological symptom disorder (conversion disorder) with weakness as the primary neuropsychiatric diagnosis, occurring in the context of significant comorbid major depressive disorder and PTSD related to childhood trauma. The current psychosocial stressors (custody dispute, job loss) have likely served as precipitants for symptom onset and escalation. The patient does not meet criteria for malingering or factitious disorder — her symptoms are not intentionally produced and she demonstrates genuine distress. Recommended treatment includes a multidisciplinary approach with specialized physiotherapy for functional motor symptoms, psychotherapy (trauma-focused CBT or EMDR for PTSD), optimization of antidepressant therapy, and gradual functional rehabilitation.
abnormal_values_summary: Functional Neurological Symptom Disorder (Conversion Disorder) with weakness

CSF_Analysis.clinical_significance: Normal; argues against GBS, inflammatory or infectious process

output.interpretation: Completely normal CSF analysis. No evidence of infection, inflammation, demyelination (no oligoclonal bands), or malignancy. Normal protein level argues against Guillain-Barré syndrome or chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy. These findings, combined with normal blood work, neuroimaging, and electrophysiology, further support a non-organic etiology for the patient's symptoms.

output.query: functional neurological disorder diagnosis positive signs criteria

results.title: Functional neurological disorder: new subtypes and shared mechanisms
results.authors: Espay AJ, Aybek S, Carson A, et al.
results.key_findings: FND should be diagnosed based on positive clinical signs (e.g., Hoover sign, tremor entrainment, distractibility) rather than solely by exclusion of organic disease.

results.title: Diagnostic criteria for functional neurological disorder: a clinical score (FND score)
results.authors: Daum C, Hubschmid M, Aybek S
results.key_findings: Proposed clinical scoring system incorporating Hoover sign, give-way weakness, inconsistency of symptoms, and psychiatric comorbidity for FND diagnosis.

results.title: Evidence-based guidelines for the management of functional neurological disorder
results.authors: Perez DL, Edwards MJ, Nielsen G, et al.
results.key_findings: Multidisciplinary treatment approach recommended including specialized physiotherapy, psychotherapy (especially CBT), and patient education. Diagnosis should be communicated transparently.

output.summary: Current evidence strongly supports diagnosing FND based on positive clinical signs rather than by exclusion. Key positive signs include Hoover sign for functional weakness, give-way or collapsing weakness, inconsistency between examined and functional movements, and distractibility. Treatment should be multidisciplinary, involving specialized physiotherapy, psychotherapy, and transparent communication of the diagnosis.

SSEP.clinical_significance: Symmetric; no evidence of central conduction delay

output.interpretation: Normal somatosensory evoked potentials from bilateral tibial nerve stimulation. Normal cortical responses with symmetric latencies and amplitudes. No evidence of central sensory pathway dysfunction. This is inconsistent with a structural spinal cord or brain lesion affecting sensory pathways and supports a non-organic etiology for the patient's reported bilateral lower extremity sensory loss.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Functional neurological disorder (conversion disorder) with weakness

differential.key_features: Family history of MS in maternal aunt raises concern, bilateral leg weakness and sensory loss could suggest myelopathy, but normal brain and spine MRI, normal CSF without oligoclonal bands, and normal evoked potentials effectively exclude this diagnosis

Progressive bilateral weakness is concerning for GBS, but preserved reflexes (2+ throughout), normal nerve conduction studies, normal CSF protein, absence of preceding infection, and 8-week time course inconsistent with typical GBS

Bilateral leg weakness with sensory level could suggest myelopathy, but normal spinal cord MRI with contrast, normal evoked potentials, preserved reflexes without upper motor neuron signs, and downgoing plantar responses exclude compressive or inflammatory myelopathy
Bilateral weakness raises concern, but no fatigable weakness pattern, no bulbar symptoms, negative AChR antibodies, and no decremental response expected on RNS

Must be considered given medicolegal context (custody dispute), but patient demonstrates genuine distress, valid performance on effort testing (TOMM, Rey 15-Item), and FND is a recognized neuropsychiatric disorder distinct from malingering

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed history including timeline of symptom progression, psychosocial stressors, and prior psychiatric history
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Progressive bilateral leg weakness over 8 weeks coinciding with major psychosocial stressors, history of fibromyalgia, depression, and childhood trauma

optimal_actions.action: Perform comprehensive neurological examination with specific attention to functional neurological signs (Hoover sign, distractibility testing, give-way weakness pattern)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Positive Hoover sign bilaterally, give-way weakness, inconsistency between volitional and automatic movements, non-dermatomal sensory loss

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI to rule out structural CNS pathology
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal brain MRI with no structural lesion, demyelination, or infarction

optimal_actions.action: Order routine EEG given reported episodes
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal EEG with no epileptiform activity

optimal_actions.action: Obtain basic laboratory workup including CBC, BMP, thyroid function, B12, inflammatory markers, and CK
optimal_actions.expected_finding: All laboratory values within normal limits

optimal_actions.action: Order MRI of cervical and thoracic spine with contrast to rule out myelopathy
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal spinal cord with no evidence of myelopathy, demyelination, or cord compression

optimal_actions.action: Order EMG/NCS of bilateral lower extremities to rule out peripheral nerve and muscle disease
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal nerve conduction studies and EMG with reduced volitional recruitment but normal MUAP morphology consistent with submaximal effort

optimal_actions.action: Request prolonged video-EEG monitoring to capture clinical events and assess for EEG correlate
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Clinical event captured without EEG correlate (PNES), preserved leg movements during sleep

optimal_actions.action: Refer for formal psychiatric evaluation to assess for comorbid psychiatric conditions
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Comorbid major depressive disorder and PTSD identified

optimal_actions.action: Communicate the diagnosis of FND to the patient in a transparent, empathetic, and non-dismissive manner, explaining the positive clinical evidence supporting the diagnosis
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Patient educated about FND as a genuine neurological condition with evidence-based treatments

optimal_actions.action: Initiate multidisciplinary treatment plan including specialized physiotherapy for functional motor symptoms, trauma-focused psychotherapy, and optimization of antidepressant medication
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Treatment plan established with physical therapy, psychology/psychiatry, and medication management

critical_actions: Perform specific functional neurological examination maneuvers (Hoover sign, distractibility testing) rather than relying on standard motor examination alone
Rule out structural and inflammatory spinal cord disease with MRI spine
Rule out peripheral nerve/muscle disease with EMG/NCS
Diagnose FND based on POSITIVE clinical signs (Hoover sign, inconsistency), not merely by exclusion of organic disease
Screen for and address comorbid psychiatric conditions (depression, PTSD, anxiety)
Communicate the diagnosis in a transparent and empathetic manner that validates the patient's symptoms as real
Distinguish FND from malingering — FND symptoms are not intentionally produced
contraindicated_actions: Dismissing the patient's symptoms as 'not real' or 'all in your head'
Continuing to order unnecessary invasive tests after adequate workup has excluded organic disease
Prescribing opioids or benzodiazepines for the functional weakness
Diagnosing by exclusion alone without identifying positive functional signs
Labeling the patient as a malingerer based solely on the presence of a custody dispute
Initiating immunosuppressive therapy (e.g., IVIG, plasma exchange) for suspected GBS without supportive electrodiagnostic or CSF findings
Recommending surgical intervention for the mild incidental degenerative disc disease
key_reasoning_points: The progressive bilateral weakness with non-dermatomal sensory loss, preserved reflexes, and downgoing plantar responses is inconsistent with upper or lower motor neuron disease

The positive Hoover sign is a 'rule-in' sign for functional weakness — it demonstrates involuntary activation of hip flexors that the patient cannot voluntarily recruit, indicating intact motor pathways
Give-way weakness (initial strong contraction followed by sudden collapse) is a positive sign of functional weakness, distinct from true pyramidal or peripheral weakness patterns
The sharp non-dermatomal sensory boundary at the waistline does not correspond to any dermatome or peripheral nerve territory, which is inconsistent with organic sensory loss
Preservation of leg movements during sleep (observed by mother and on video-EEG) demonstrates intact motor pathways and is inconsistent with structural weakness
The extensive negative workup (normal MRI brain and spine, normal EMG/NCS, normal labs, normal CSF) excludes the major organic differential diagnoses

The temporal correlation with major psychosocial stressors (custody dispute, job loss) and background of childhood trauma and psychiatric comorbidity provides important context but is not required for diagnosis

This case is a diagnostic puzzle because: (1) the initial presentation mimics myelopathy or GBS, (2) the Hoover sign was not detected on initial bedside exam, (3) the family history of MS in a maternal aunt is a red herring, (4) valid effort on performance testing argues against malingering, and (5) the diagnosis requires a neurologist experienced in recognizing positive functional signs
FND is a diagnosis of inclusion based on positive clinical signs, not a diagnosis of exclusion — modern diagnostic criteria emphasize this distinction

metadata.difficulty_rationale: Diagnostic puzzle due to progressive bilateral weakness mimicking myelopathy or GBS, absent initial positive functional signs, family history of MS as red herring, and need for specialized examination maneuvers to make positive diagnosis
